events.title: The First Footprint Off the Sun
events.scene: It took mastering the whole solar system to get here: the Jovian moons cracked for hydrogen, the Belt strip-mined for metal and water, baking foundries on Mercury and floating cities over Venus, all of it feeding the deep-space shipyards that finally throw something past the heliopause. A crewed sliver of metal rides a fusion-pulse drive at a hard-won few percent of light, the first humans to leave the Sun's gravity well entirely. What you do not yet know is that a ship under power, leaking neutrons and radio chatter, is the loudest thing a quiet galaxy has heard in ten thousand years.
events.researchNote: Interstellar capability is downstream of in-system industrialization (Jovian volatiles, Belt metals, Mercury solar foundries, Venusian aerostats); a powered, radiating fusion-pulse drive at low-single-digit percent of c is far more detectable than a passive planet, the realistic trigger for being 'noticed.'

choices.text: Declare the whole sky a Trump-branded frontier.
choices.outcome: You name the empty dark after yourself before anyone can object. The scientists wince; the brand soars.

choices.text: Quietly triple the budget for who might be listening back.
choices.outcome: You authorize the largest radio-listening array in human history. If something heard the engine, you want to hear it first.

choices.text: Tell the public it is just a fancy rocket, nothing more.
choices.outcome: The headlines move on. Inside the war room, the physicists keep a private, growing column titled 'detection radius.'

choices.text: Beam a victory address at full power toward the nearest stars.
choices.outcome: Your face and voice now ride outward at light-speed, an unencrypted boast aimed at everyone who has been keeping silent for very good reasons.

events.title: The Dark Forest Cabinet Meeting
events.scene: Your strategists lay out the cold logic. The galaxy is silent not because it is empty but possibly because everyone with a transmitter learned to stay quiet, every civilization a hunter crouched in a dark forest, and the first to shout draws the first shot. The ship already shouted. The only choice left is whether to shout louder or go dark and pray the signal hasn't landed somewhere with teeth.
events.researchNote: The dark-forest hypothesis (Liu Cixin) frames interstellar silence as mutual fear under irreversible light-speed lag; broadcasting is a one-way, unrecallable commitment.

choices.text: Kill every beacon. Make Earth a hole in the sky.
choices.outcome: You order the lights doused across the system. If the forest is full of hunters, you intend to be the bush no one walks toward.

choices.text: Broadcast harder. Let them fear the loudest predator first.
choices.outcome: Your instinct is the bully's instinct: don't hide from the forest, become its biggest animal. The transmitters scream your strength outward.

choices.text: Send one careful, peaceful greeting. No threats, just math.
choices.outcome: Against your gut, you let the scientists draft a measured hello in prime numbers and goodwill. It leaves Earth carrying our better face.

choices.text: Send nothing. Just listen and wait for the lag.
choices.outcome: You choose patience, a word that tastes strange in your mouth. The dishes turn outward and the long, agonizing wait begins.

events.title: The Speed of Light Is Not Negotiable
events.scene: Your engineers explain, again, that even the nearest stars are years away at the speed of the only messenger that exists. Any answer to the ship, or to any signal, is locked behind a wall of decades; you could die of old age before a 'hello' completes its round trip. For a man used to instant cable rebuttals, a conversation measured in human lifetimes is its own special torture.
events.researchNote: Round-trip light delay to even nearby stars is years to decades; no signaling or causal influence exceeds c, so interstellar 'dialogue' is generational by physical law.

choices.text: Order them to find a loophole in the speed of light.
choices.outcome: You cannot fire causality, so you fire three lab directors instead. The light still takes its years, indifferent to your title.

choices.text: Fund a century-long listening institution to outlive you.
choices.outcome: You endow a body designed to wait across generations, the first thing you have ever built that admits you will not see the end of it.

choices.text: Stage a fake 'they answered us' broadcast to win the news cycle.
choices.outcome: You manufacture aliens for ratings. When the real signal eventually comes, no one will know which Trump to believe.

events.title: Something in the Static
events.scene: The listening array catches it at three in the morning, Mars time: a structured signal, prime numbers folding into a repeating frame, beamed from the direction the ship was heading. It is unmistakably artificial and unmistakably aimed. The whole apparatus of human civilization now hangs on a single question you are not equipped to answer: what does it say?
events.researchNote: A deliberately decodable first message would likely lead with mathematical universals (primes, pi) before any linguistic content, the standard SETI assumption for establishing intentionality.

choices.text: Lock the world's best minds in a room until it's decoded.
choices.outcome: You assemble the smartest people alive and, for once, let them work without your improvements. The frame slowly begins to yield.

choices.text: Classify it. The signal is now your private property.
choices.outcome: You stamp the most important message in history TOP SECRET and TRUMP ONLY. The galaxy's first words to humanity become a bargaining chip.

choices.text: Treat it as an attack. Put the system on war footing.
choices.outcome: You mobilize fleets against a string of prime numbers. Fear, as always, is the easiest thing in the system to scale.

choices.text: Tell the whole human species at once. They have a right to know.
choices.outcome: For one night, eleven billion humans look up at the same patch of sky. Whatever happens next, they face it together and they know your name said it first.

events.title: No Rosetta Stone in the Sky
events.scene: Decoding the math was the easy part; meaning is the wall. The senders share no biology, no history, no body to ground a single metaphor, and your translators warn that a mistranslated nuance could read as either a blessing or a declaration of war. The aliens, whoever they are, built the message assuming a mind utterly unlike yours would try to read it. How you choose to read it back will color everything.
events.researchNote: Linguistic anchoring requires shared referents; with no shared embodiment, translation must bootstrap from physics and mathematics, and tone/intent is the hardest layer to recover.

choices.text: Instruct the team to read every ambiguity as goodwill.
choices.outcome: You bet on the better reading. The reply Earth drafts comes back warm, open, and unarmed, and that is the face the senders will judge.

choices.text: Instruct the team to read every ambiguity as a threat.
choices.outcome: You choose suspicion as policy. Earth's reply hardens into a warning shot in syntax, and the senders will answer the threat they were shown.

choices.text: Hand the whole exchange to a neutral machine intelligence.
choices.outcome: You cede first contact to an algorithm with no ego and no temper, which is, your advisors note dryly, an improvement on the alternative.

events.title: You Get the Neighbor You Deserve
events.scene: Your xeno-strategists deliver an uncomfortable theory: the galaxy is not monolithic, and which civilization deigns to make contact depends on how Earth has presented itself across decades of leaking signal. A species reads our broadcasts the way a stranger reads a face. Show the cosmos a hand, and a hand may reach back; show it a fist, and something with bigger fists will come to test it.
events.researchNote: A 'reputation-selects-the-respondent' framing: a civilization's long-baseline broadcast profile (peaceful science vs. martial chest-beating) plausibly filters which kind of distant actor finds engagement worthwhile.

choices.text: Reshape every outbound signal into an offer of friendship.
choices.outcome: You scrub the menace out of Earth's voice and let it sing in good faith. The forest, for once, may answer with something kind.

choices.text: Project pure strength. Let them know Earth bites.
choices.outcome: You broadcast teeth. Somewhere out there, something with more teeth notes the challenge and begins the long approach.

choices.text: Send peace publicly, threats privately. Hedge the species.
choices.outcome: You try to be both lamb and wolf on the same frequency. The senders, reading both, decide to come close enough to find out which is real.

events.title: The Concord Answers
events.scene: After years of patient, peaceful broadcasting, a reply unfolds across the array, gentle and astonishingly clear: they call themselves the Concord, a federation of old species who watch the dark forest and quietly extend a hand to anyone who shows one first. They have read Earth's better face for decades and judged it worth the risk. They are coming not to conquer but to talk, and they have noticed that this fragile species is led by a man who very much enjoys the spotlight.
events.researchNote: Benevolent-respondent branch gated on a peaceful broadcast posture; 'the Concord' is the original in-world benevolent polity, replacing any franchise analog.

choices.text: Welcome the Concord openly as humanity's friends.
choices.outcome: You stand before the species and welcome the Concord with open arms, the most statesmanlike thing you have ever done. First contact is made, and it is peace.

choices.text: Smile for the cameras, but squeeze them for a deal first.
choices.outcome: You turn first contact into a negotiation, and somehow the ancient pacifists agree to put your name on the treaty. Contact is made, on your terms.

choices.text: Stall. No species is this nice without an angle.
choices.outcome: You shake the Concord's hand with one of yours while the other stays on the launch keys. Contact is made, but you keep the safety off.

events.title: Something With Teeth Arrives
events.scene: Years of chest-beating broadcasts drew exactly what you advertised for. The signal that finally resolves is not a greeting but a scan, cold and total, and behind it comes a fleet that treats Earth's defiance as an invitation. They are predators by economy and design, and they have come because a loud, hostile, resource-rich little system practically begged to be visited. The forest answered the fist with a bigger one.
events.researchNote: Malevolent-respondent branch gated on a predatory/high-heat broadcast posture; consistent with dark-forest logic where aggressive signaling selects for aggressive contact.

choices.text: Throw the unified war machine of Earth and Mars at them.
choices.outcome: You hurl everything humanity has into the void. Contact is made the only way you ever truly understood it: as a war you intend to win.

choices.text: Sue for terms before a shot is fired.
choices.outcome: You raise the white flag before the enemy even asks for one. Contact is made, and humanity's first word to its conqueror is 'please.'

choices.text: Privately offer to govern Earth on the invaders' behalf.
choices.outcome: Where others see an apocalypse, you see a new boss to flatter. You make contact by offering to sell your own planet a tenant at a time.

events.title: The Gift of Folded Space
events.scene: The Concord shares their crowning technology: a drive that folds the geometry of space itself, collapsing the cruel light-speed barrier that has tormented you for two decades into a thing you can step across. They offer it freely, as a teacher offers a pencil, with one condition you find baffling, that humanity use it to talk and trade rather than to raid. Suddenly the entire galaxy is no longer years away but reachable, and the question of what kind of empire you build out there becomes very real.
events.researchNote: Benevolent FTL-gift branch ('warp_gift'); the in-world drive is a metric-folding mechanism described in original terms, not a franchise reference.

choices.text: Accept the drive and honor the terms of peace.
choices.outcome: You take the pencil and, against every instinct, agree to write rather than stab. Humanity inherits the stars as a citizen, not a conqueror.

choices.text: Take the drive now, plan to break the terms later.
choices.outcome: You pocket the gift while already drafting the doublecross. The Concord trusts you; you have never let trust go to waste unexploited.

choices.text: Open-source the drive to every nation and colony at once.
choices.outcome: You give the stars to everyone, an act so generous your advisors check you for a fever. History records it as the day humanity grew up.

events.title: Two Immortals, One Alien Phone Line
events.scene: Elon Musk is the only other man who refused to die, and the centuries have made you less partners than rival gods squabbling over one prize: who owns the relationship with whatever is out there. His foundries built the ships; your unified Earth gave them somewhere to launch from, and now the alien signal has arrived to a system with two immortal egos and exactly one channel to the stars. He wants the alien tech routed through his companies; you want it routed through your throne. Only one name can be on the first interstellar handshake.
events.researchNote: Power-dynamics motive after history ends: two functionally immortal actors create a centuries-long control struggle over the singular, civilization-defining alien contact channel.

choices.text: Nationalize the contact channel. The state, meaning you, owns it.
choices.outcome: You wrench the alien line out of Musk's hands and into yours. The other immortal smiles the smile of a man who plays a very long game.

choices.text: Cut Musk in. Two immortals are stronger than one against aliens.
choices.outcome: You swallow your pride and share the prize. Humanity meets the galaxy fronted by its two deathless founders, an uneasy duumvirate of titans.

choices.text: Quietly sabotage his foundries so only your ships fly out.
choices.outcome: You knife the only man who could match you, slowing humanity's reach to guarantee your grip on it. The feud will outlive empires.

events.title: The War for the Solar System
events.scene: The fighting is nothing like the movies: it is fusion fire across light-seconds, decoys and railguns and the brutal arithmetic of who can absorb more loss. Earth and Mars, unified under your iron will, throw decades of military buildup at the predator fleet, and for once your appetite for total domination is exactly the instinct the moment demands. Whether humanity ends this as victor or chattel depends on the next order you give.
events.researchNote: Interplanetary combat realism: light-second engagement ranges, kinetic/directed-energy weapons, attrition logistics; outcome branches seed Era 12 power structure.

choices.text: Crush their fleet and seize their ships, their world, their kind.
choices.outcome: Humanity wins, and you do not stop at winning. The survivors of the predator fleet are marched in chains into your factories. An empire of slaves is born.

choices.text: Fight to the last and lose. Earth falls under their boot.
choices.outcome: Courage was not enough. The predator fleet breaks Earth's defenses and humanity wakes up to find it is no longer the owner of its own sky.

choices.text: Bleed them to a standstill and carve up the system.
choices.outcome: Neither side can finish the other. The war freezes into a poisoned border across the asteroid belt, and you rule a fortress instead of a planet.

events.title: The Tenant Emperor
events.scene: The predators accept your private overture with cold amusement: a native administrator who will keep the population docile and the resource quotas flowing is cheaper than a garrison. They offer you a throne that is really a desk, a crown that is really a collar, and the chance to rule humanity as the friendly face of its own harvest. It is the worst deal in the history of the species, and it has your name written all over it.
events.researchNote: Quisling/collaborator branch ('quisling'); a local proxy administrator is a low-cost occupation strategy, seeding the subjugated-Earth Era 12 path.

choices.text: Take the throne-that-is-a-collar. A king is still a king.
choices.outcome: You sign humanity into bondage and call yourself its emperor anyway. You become the puppet on the throne, beloved of no one, owned by the stars.

choices.text: Pretend to collaborate, then turn their own ships on them.
choices.outcome: You play the loyal puppet just long enough to get your hands on the strings, then cut their throats with them. Betrayal, at last, aimed up instead of down.

choices.text: Spit on the offer. Lead humanity's doomed resistance instead.
choices.outcome: For the first time, you refuse the easy con. You go down swinging as the figurehead of a resistance you will probably not survive, and the species loves you for it.

events.title: Xenobiology and the Quarantine Question
events.scene: Whoever is coming, they carry a biochemistry no Earth immune system has ever rehearsed against, and your epidemiologists are pale with worry over what an exchange of microbes could do in both directions. There is no precedent, no vaccine, no second chance if a sealed lab leaks. The science is screaming for caution while your politics scream for a triumphant handshake on live broadcast.
events.researchNote: Xenobiological cross-contamination risk is symmetric and unpredictable absent shared evolutionary history; rigorous quarantine is the realistic first-contact protocol.

choices.text: No contact without a hardened orbital quarantine first.
choices.outcome: You build a sterile gate between two biospheres and refuse to open it until the science says safe. Boring, unglamorous, and exactly right.

choices.text: Skip the lab. Shake the alien's hand on prime-time.
choices.outcome: You get the photo of the century and gamble eleven billion immune systems for it. The ratings are spectacular; the epidemiologists are praying.

choices.text: Meet only through sealed robots until biology is understood.
choices.outcome: First contact happens through telepresence and steel proxies, no flesh touching flesh. Cold, careful, and survivable.

events.title: The Markets Meet Infinity
events.scene: Contact does to the economy what gravity does to a dropped glass. If a friendly power can fold space, scarcity itself looks negotiable; if a hostile one looms, every market prices in the apocalypse overnight. Currencies built on Earthly scarcity convulse, and you, the man who has spent a lifetime monetizing everything, must decide whether to corner the new reality or steady it.
events.researchNote: Contact-economics shock: post-scarcity technology or existential threat both detonate scarcity-priced markets; the power transfer is the real story of contact.

choices.text: Corner every scrap of alien tech and license it back.
choices.outcome: You move to own the future the way you once owned casinos. A galactic monopoly forms with your name on the deed and a target on its back.

choices.text: Cushion the crash. Manage the species through the shock.
choices.outcome: You spend treasure to keep eleven billion people from panic, an investment in stability you would once have called weakness. It holds.

choices.text: Short the whole planet and buy the wreckage cheap.
choices.outcome: You bet against your own species' nerve and win obscenely. The richest man in two worlds, hated in both.

events.title: Eleven Billion Frightened People
events.scene: The species is terrified, and terror is the one market you have always understood best. They want to be told who to fear, who to follow, and that everything will be tremendous. Standing before every screen in the solar system, you can soothe humanity into courage or stoke it into a weapon aimed at the stars, and your instinct, as ever, leans toward the crowd that chants.
events.researchNote: Mass-psychology beat: existential fear is maximally manipulable; the leader's choice shapes whether the species meets contact as a polity or a mob.

choices.text: Give the speech of your life and steady the human race.
choices.outcome: You find, somewhere, the better angel of your showmanship, and eleven billion heart rates slow at the sound of your voice. It may be your finest hour.

choices.text: Whip up xenophobic panic and ride it to absolute power.
choices.outcome: You feed the fear instead of facing it, and it lifts you to a throne above a frightened, furious species pointed like a gun at the sky.

choices.text: Let the scientists explain it. Stay above the panic.
choices.outcome: For once you cede the podium to people who actually understand the thing. The species is calmer; your ego, less so.

events.title: What the War Made of You
events.scene: The fighting is over and the shape of the new order hardens around you. Whether humanity won, lost, or sold itself, the galaxy is now a place where your name means something, and the choices you make in this charred peace will define the empire that carries forward into the stars. The dark forest knows Earth's face now, for better or for worse, and that face is largely yours.
events.researchNote: Capstone consolidation of the hostile branch; routes the player's war outcome cleanly into Era 12's power structure flags.

choices.text: Build a permanent order on the back of the conquered.
choices.outcome: You forge the spoils of contact into a machine of permanent dominance. Humanity goes to the stars as a master, and you hold the leash.

choices.text: Free the survivors and seek an uneasy coexistence.
choices.outcome: You do the unthinkable and open the cages. It costs you raw power and buys humanity something it will need out there: the chance not to be hated.

choices.text: Let a puppet front the empire while you pull every string.
choices.outcome: You learn the oldest trick of empire: take the power, give away the blame. The star-empire's true owner is a name on no document.

events.title: The Empire That Cannot Be Ruled
events.scene: Your strategists put the cruelest truth of your immortal reign on the table: even with folded space, the colonies you send outward will be years of light-lag away, and an order takes longer to arrive than a rebellion takes to win. You can rule a small empire absolutely, every world inside a tight light-hour of your throne, or you can fling humanity across the galaxy and watch your governors become kings the moment they pass the horizon. Distance, not death, is the one tyrant even an immortal cannot conquer.
events.researchNote: Relativistic separation makes real-time central authority physically impossible across light-years; the immortal autocrat's core dilemma is control (small, absolute) vs. expansion (vast, ungovernable).

choices.text: Keep the empire small enough to hold in your fist.
choices.outcome: You choose the iron grip over the wide reach, a jewel-box empire ruled to the second. Humanity stays leashed, and the far stars stay forever someone else's.

choices.text: Fling humanity outward and accept you cannot rule it all.
choices.outcome: You let the species scatter to a thousand suns knowing your governors will become rivals within a generation. You trade control for immortality of a different kind: legacy.

choices.text: Send your cult outward in your place. Let belief be the leash.
choices.outcome: If you cannot send orders fast enough, you send faith instead, a worship of your name that needs no signal to enforce. Distant worlds genuflect to a god they will never see move.

events.title: The Door to the Galaxy
events.scene: Whatever path brought you here, humanity now stands on a threshold no generation before could even imagine, with the means to cross the dark and a reputation, kind or cruel, already preceding it. The Sun shrinks behind the first fold-ships as they vanish toward a thousand suns. You have made first contact and survived it, and the only question left is what kind of master, partner, or monster Earth becomes among the stars.
events.researchNote: Era capstone funneling all branches toward Era 12 (Interstellar); requires contact_made, which is the documented entry gate for the next era.

choices.text: Take humanity to the stars under your unbroken banner.
choices.outcome: You step through the door first, banner in hand, and the species follows. The age of one Sun ends; the age of your empire among many begins.

choices.text: Hand the future to humanity and take your bow.
choices.outcome: In a final, shocking act of restraint, you give the galaxy to the people rather than to yourself. History, at last, struggles to call you small.

choices.text: Make the stars a gated property with one owner: you.
choices.outcome: You cross the threshold and immediately put up a velvet rope and a price list. The infinite becomes, somehow, just another Trump property.

events.title: The Solar Crucible
events.scene: Before we can listen to the stars, we must strip-mine the sun. Your mining swarms on Mercury are melting entire ridges to forge the mirror arrays for the grand deep-space listening project. The laborers, roasting behind magnetic heat-shields, are demanding triple-hazard pay as solar flares lick their orbit.
events.researchNote: Early 22nd-century deep-space arrays required unprecedented radio-shielding and raw metallic mass, sourced directly from inner-system bodies.

choices.text: Deny the hazard pay; let the magnetic shields run hot to meet the arrays' deadline.
choices.outcome: The mirrors are aligned on schedule, but the graveyard shifts on Mercury are now literal graveyards. Whispers of unionized sabotage begin.

choices.text: Sell early transmission options to private terrestrial cartels to offset the cost.
choices.outcome: Private capital floods in, compromising your exclusive hold over the system's finest ear.

choices.text: Divert the harvested nickel-iron to fortify our orbital private sanctuaries instead.
choices.outcome: The listening project slows, but your private bunkers are now thick enough to survive a direct solar storm or a kinetic strike.

events.title: The Dark Forest Schism
events.scene: The mathematical signal is a mess of undecipherable prime structures. In the family drawing room, your heirs split over what to beam back into the dark. One side demands we broadcast a terrifying demonstration of our nuclear arsenals; the other begs to send a registry of our greatest cultural accomplishments, hoping for mercy.
events.researchNote: Without a shared semantic framework, initial interstellar communications are more likely to project the sender's own anxieties than to convey actual meaning.

choices.text: Side with the reactionaries. Broadcast our tactical capabilities to show we are not prey.
choices.outcome: The galaxy now knows us by our weapons. The peaceful factions of the solar system look upon your house with horror.

choices.text: Side with the reformers. Send our collective art, music, and scientific databases.
choices.outcome: The cultural registry is sent. The public applauds your humanist vision, though your defense chiefs mutter that you have handed predators our map.

choices.text: Order absolute transmitter silence. Sever the family's public link to the array.
choices.outcome: The array goes quiet. Your family retreats into private security, leaving the rest of the species to make their own noise.

events.title: The Temple of the Coming Light
events.scene: Eleven billion people are screaming for salvation as alien ships are confirmed en route. Seizing the moment, your younger cousin has founded a lucrative media ministry, selling 'Bunkered Salvation Passes' and luxury fallout chambers on the moon, promising that your family's technology is the only vessel of survival.
events.researchNote: In moments of existential civilizational panic, catastrophic millenarian movements consistently monetize fear through the sale of physical or spiritual sanctuary.

choices.text: Integrate the ministry into the family brand and maximize the sale of orbital tickets.
choices.outcome: The vault overflows with the life savings of terrified middle-class Terrans. Your name is now synonymous with divine exploitation.

choices.text: Weaponize the cult. Declare our family the divinely ordained defenders against the alien menace.
choices.outcome: Armed zealots patrol your estates, ready to die for your bloodline. The planetary authorities look on with extreme apprehension.

choices.text: Shut down the cult and build state-sponsored public shelters under our corporate logo.
choices.outcome: You lose a fortune in potential tithes, but the local populations bless your name as they file into the safety of the concrete deep.

events.title: The Smuggled Fold
events.scene: The Concord's folding-space drives are intended for scientific exploration, but their components are highly volatile and extremely illegal to reproduce. Your black-market shipping networks have managed to divert several exotic matter cores from the joint interstellar assembly bays. This could break your dependence on public transit forever.
events.researchNote: The transfer of highly complex, dual-use alien technology invariably sparks black-market copycatting and illicit distribution networks.

choices.text: Sell the stolen folding cores to rogue outer-system mining cartels for a premium.
choices.outcome: You are filthy rich, but the UN Security Council is now tracking the radioactive signatures of your shipping lanes.

choices.text: Launder the foreign tech through our domestic research labs, claiming a patent breakthrough.
choices.outcome: The planetary courts confirm your 'genius patent,' elevating your family to the premier engine-wrights of the solar system.

choices.text: Hoard the cores in our family's deep-space bunkers for our exclusive survival ships.
choices.outcome: Our private yachts are now faster than anything in the public navy. We have our escape route, if the worst should happen.

events.title: The Price of Administrative Grace
events.scene: As the officially appointed 'Tenant Emperors' under our new alien overlords, your house has been handed a brutal quota: three million metric tons of heavy water from the Jovian moons, and a draft of one hundred thousand labor-conscripts. Earth's remaining free unions have called a general strike.
events.researchNote: Collaborationist regimes historically maintain their position by enforcing extraction quotas on their own populations to satisfy their conquerors.

choices.text: Deploy our private security forces to break the strike on Ganymede with force.
choices.outcome: The fuel flows to the conqueror's vessels. Your family's name is spat upon in every worker's barracks from Earth to Neptune.

choices.text: Falsify the export ledgers and secretly divert part of the raw water to Earth's agricultural reserves.
choices.outcome: The underground thanks you, but the alien inspectors are not fools; they suspect someone is skimming the system's lifeblood.

choices.text: Blame the delivery delays on remaining Martian saboteurs, directing the alien purges toward them.
choices.outcome: The invaders clear out your political rivals on Mars for you. You remain the indispensable local manager.

events.title: The Collaborator's Disgrace
events.scene: The war is over, and the public has discovered the extent of your eldest child's intimate cooperation with the alien occupation forces during the 'Tenant Emperor' years. The family's public standing is in ruins. A younger, long-ignored sibling who spent the war in isolation has been brought forward to rescue the family brand.
events.researchNote: Post-occupation transitions often require the symbolic sacrifice of frontline collaborators in favor of untainted cadet branches.

choices.text: Publicly exile the collaborator heir to a remote asteroid and install the quiet sibling.
choices.outcome: The public is mollified by the clean-cut new patriarch, though the exiled sibling swears to take their revenge from the dark.

choices.text: Keep the old heir and fund an expensive media campaign reframing their work as secret resistance.
choices.outcome: History is written by the rich. The propaganda is transparent, but it holds the line against outright expropriation.

choices.text: Donate our heavily criticized Martian assets to the post-war reconstruction committee.
choices.outcome: You lose a massive revenue stream, but you buy the survival of the dynasty in the new era.

events.title: The Rogue Ark
events.scene: With the galaxy's door now open but light-lag threatening to cut off any distant holdings, your youngest branch has decided to take half of the family's liquid treasury and board an independent colony ship destined for the Gliese system. They intend to establish a new dynasty, completely free of your iron grip.
events.researchNote: The advent of interstellar migration offered cadet branches the ultimate escape hatch to flee domestic authority and establish independent lines.

choices.text: Lock down their colony ship's engines through our system-wide software overrides.
choices.outcome: The defectors are trapped on Earth, bitter and desperate, their dreams of independence crushed under your boot.

choices.text: Allow them to depart, on the condition that they legally renounce all future claims to the home estate.
choices.outcome: The ark departs into the cold night. Your ancestral coffers are lighter, but the line remains clean and unified back home.

choices.text: Fund their journey publicly and write it off as an interstellar development grant.
choices.outcome: The public admires your generosity to the pioneers, while your accountants smile at the massive tax relief achieved on the back of family division.
